1.1 Objectives and ambition We spend over €1 trillion annually on construction in Europe, yet billions are lost each year to maintenance, premature degradation, and non-recyclable materials. MOSAIC addresses this systemic inefficiency by enabling a new construction paradigm: one that combines modular off-site prefabrication with embedded intelligence, rapid on-site assembly through stimuli-responsive reversible connections, and autonomous structural health monitoring (SHM) that delivers prescriptive maintenance and real-time self-healing. Our overall goal is to tackle the fact that the European construction sector is responsible for 36% of EU greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and over 35% of total waste. It urgently needs materials that are high-performance, intelligent, and circular. Fibre-Reinforced Polymer Composites (FRPCs) offer exceptional strength-to-weight ratios that uniquely enable large structural modules to be prefabricated in controlled factory environments, transported as finished elements, and rapidly assembled on site, fundamentally reducing on-site labour, construction time, and logistical complexity compared with steel-reinforced concrete. However, two barriers prevent FRPCs from fulfilling this potential: (i) irreversible thermoset crosslinks render components non-recyclable and non-repairable; and (ii) the absence of embedded intelligence prevents adaptive in-service response. We eliminate both barriers by engineering a new class of FRPCs based on unsaturated polyester (UP)/vinyl ester (VE) resins with dynamic covalent bonds, adaptable crosslinked systems (vitrimers) that retain the mechanical performance of conventional thermosets under service conditions but unlock reprocessing, self-healing, and reversible assembly under controlled thermal activation, while embedding cost-effective resistive sensors and thermal actuators directly into the composite architecture. The result is a construction material lightweight enough to be prefabricated as large modules, transported to site, and rapidly assembled through reversible adhesive connections that are structurally rigid in service yet enable clean separation for maintenance, reconfiguration, or reuse, and that, once deployed, monitors its own health, heals its own damage, and thereby extends service life, and can ultimately be disassembled, repaired, or recycled at end of life (EoL). These reversible joints provide rigid structural bonding during service and on-demand debonding under controlled thermal activation. Embedded resistive sensors and distributed thermal actuators, integrated via Tailored Fibre Placement (TFP), form a self-monitoring system: damage detection triggers localized self-healing through dynamic bond exchange, restoring structural integrity without manual intervention. A prescriptive Digital Twin (DT) orchestrates the sensing-analysis-actuation loop, fusing multi-physics modelling with sensor data within an edge-cloud architecture compliant with EU AI Act and cybersecurity standards, moving beyond predictive monitoring to prescriptive maintenance where the DT autonomously prescribes and initiates repair. Both vitrimer resins and wire-based sensor/actuator systems are designed as low-cost solutions compatible with existing pultrusion and Liquid Composite Moulding (LCM) processing, including Vacuum Assisted Resin Infusion (VARI) and Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM), delivering order-of-magnitude cost reductions compared to state-of-the-art vitrimers and fibre-optic sensing. Validation encompasses accelerated durability testing, the 6R framework, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) based on EN 15804+A2, and Design-for-Disassembly benchmarking against ISO 59020, with TRL6 pilot validation in bridge and building infrastructure alongside TRL3-5 research activities. The Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) framework and Social Sciences and Humanities (SSH) approaches, including co-creation webinars and structured acceptance metrics, are integrated from the outset to ensure that safety, sustainability, and societal needs drive material design throughout the innovation cycle, with replication potential for housing assessed through dedicated co-creation activities. As can be seen from figure 1, MOSAIC delivers an integrated system linking materials, manufacturing, and AI to enable intelligent, self-healing, and circular construction. Figure 1- Overview of the MOSAIC concept MOSAIC delivers a set of breakthroughs across materials, manufacturing, and digital intelligence for Europe’s Open Strategic Autonomy, establishing a new paradigm for circular and intelligent construction systems (Table 1). Table 1- MOSAIC Breakthroughs aligned with Europe’s open strategic autonomy innovation domains 1 Materials Sovereignty. We will create the first industrially viable vitrimer system for construction FRPCs: UP/VE resins with dynamic covalent exchange, halogen-free flame retardants with validated pathway to B-s1,d0 classification, and low-toxicity catalysts/stabilisers eliminating dependence on non-recyclable thermosets and hazardous additives. Advanced Manufacturing: We will achieve in-situ integration of sensor/actuator networks during composite manufacturing (TFP for LCM, co-processing for pultrusion), combined with stimuli-responsive reversible (dis)assembly, enabling modular off-site fabrication and rapid on-site erection of reconfigurable structural systems. Digital Intelligence: We will develop metal wire-based resistive sensors/actuators integrated at manufacturing cost orders of magnitude below fibre-optic alternatives, coupled with a physics-informed prescriptive DT that moves beyond monitoring to autonomous decision-making and self-healing activation. Circular Economy: We will implement a full 6R implementation framework validated against ISO 59020 circularity metrics, supported by a Digital Product Passport (DPP) tracking production, constitution & maintenance data across component lifecycle. Resilience & Autonomy: We will create self-aware structural components capable of in-service self-healing via dynamic bond exchange, prescriptive maintenance via AI-driven DT, and off-site repair through vitrimer reprocessability - a closed-loop resilience system with no precedent in construction composites. 1.1 Objectives and ambition We spend over €1 trillion annually on construction in Europe, yet billions are lost each year to maintenance, premature degradation, and non-recyclable materials. MOSAIC addresses this systemic inefficiency by enabling a new construction paradigm: one that combines modular off-site prefabrication with embedded intelligence, rapid on-site assembly through stimuli-responsive reversible connections, and autonomous structural health monitoring (SHM) that delivers prescriptive maintenance and real-time self-healing. Our overall goal: The European construction sector is responsible for 36% of EU greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and over 35% of total waste. It urgently needs materials that are high-performance, intelligent, and circular. Fibre-Reinforced Polymer Composites (FRPCs) offer exceptional strength-to-weight ratios that uniquely enable large structural modules to be prefabricated in controlled factory environments, transported as finished elements, and rapidly assembled on site, fundamentally reducing on-site labour, construction time, and logistical complexity compared with steel-reinforced concrete. However, two barriers prevent FRPCs from fulfilling this potential: (i) irreversible thermoset crosslinks render components non-recyclable and non-repairable; and (ii) the absence of embedded intelligence prevents adaptive in-service response. MOSAIC eliminates both barriers by engineering a new class of FRPCs based on unsaturated polyester (UP)/vinyl ester (VE) resins with dynamic covalent bonds, adaptable crosslinked systems (vitrimers) that retain the mechanical performance of conventional thermosets under service conditions but unlock reprocessing, self-healing, and reversible assembly under controlled thermal activation, while embedding cost-effective resistive sensors and thermal actuators directly into the composite architecture. The result is a construction material lightweight enough to be prefabricated as large modules, transported to site, and rapidly assembled through reversible adhesive connections that are structurally rigid in service yet enable clean separation for maintenance, reconfiguration, or reuse, and that, once deployed, monitors its own health, heals its own damage, and thereby extends service life, & can ultimately be disassembled, repaired, or recycled at end of life (EoL). These reversible joints provide rigid structural bonding during service and on-demand debonding under controlled thermal activation. Embedded resistive sensors and distributed thermal actuators, integrated via Tailored Fibre Placement (TFP), form a self-monitoring system: damage detection triggers localized self-healing through dynamic bond exchange, restoring structural integrity without manual intervention. A prescriptive Digital Twin (DT) orchestrates the sensing-analysis-actuation loop, fusing multi-physics modelling with sensor data within an edge-cloud architecture compliant with EU AI Act and cybersecurity standards, moving beyond predictive monitoring to prescriptive maintenance where the DT autonomously prescribes and initiates repair. Both vitrimer resins and wire-based sensor/actuator systems are designed as low-cost solutions compatible with existing pultrusion and Liquid Composite Moulding (LCM) processing, including Vacuum Assisted Resin Infusion (VARI) and Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM), delivering order-of-magnitude cost reductions compared to state-of-the-art vitrimers and fibre-optic sensing. Validation encompasses accelerated durability testing, the 6R framework, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) based on EN 15804+A2, and Design-for-Disassembly benchmarking against ISO 59020, with TRL6 pilot validation in bridge and building infrastructure alongside TRL3-5 research activities. The Safe and Sustainable by Design (SSbD) framework and Social Sciences and Humanities (SSH) approaches, including co-creation webinars and structured acceptance metrics, are integrated from the outset to ensure that safety, sustainability, and societal needs drive material design throughout the innovation cycle, with replication potential for housing assessed through dedicated co-creation activities. As can be seen from figure 1, MOSAIC delivers an integrated system linking materials, manufacturing, and AI to enable intelligent, self-healing, and circular construction. Advanced Manufacturing: In-situ integration of sensor/actuator networks during composite manufacturing (TFP for LCM, co-processing for pultrusion), combined with stimuli-responsive reversible (dis)assembly, enabling modular off-site fabrication and rapid on-site erection of reconfigurable structural systems. Digital Intelligence: Metal wire-based resistive sensors/actuators integrated at manufacturing cost orders of magnitude below fibre-optic alternatives, coupled with a physics-informed prescriptive DT that moves beyond monitoring to autonomous decision-making and self-healing activation. 1.1 Objectives and ambition  
1.1.1 Objectives of RePaintIT  
We recognize that paint manufacturing and consumption generate an exceptionally complex waste stream. The associated environmental and regulatory challenges are exacerbated by the stringent requirements of the EU Biocidal Products Regulation (BPR, Regulation (EU) No 528/2012) and its latest Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/86. These regulations have reduced the list of approved in-can preservatives to just 18 active substances, significantly compromising the microbial stability of waterborne formulations. As a result, our paints are now highly susceptible to bacterial and fungal contamination, particularly in warmer Southern European climates. According to CEPE (the European Council for Paint, Printing Ink, and Artists' Colours Industry), we spoil up to 11 million buckets of waterborne paint each year, representing 25% of total production - resulting in €470 million annual financial losses. [1] These figures underscore the urgent need for circular, climate-resilient waste strategies across the paint value chain. Consumer behavior further compounds the challenge. In the UK alone, we stockpile over 50 million liters of unused or partially used paint in households, 98% of which we ultimately incinerate or landfill.  
1 Campaign: Coatings need preservatives - The European Council of the Paint, Printing Ink, and Artist’s Colours Industry (CEPE)  
HORIZON-CL4-2025-05-TWIN-TRANSITION-35-two-stage — Developing and embedding upcycling technologies into viable business (Processes4Planet partnership) (IA) - RePaintIT - EU Grants: Application form (HE RIA and IA): V1.0 – 19.11.2025 Part B - Page 2 of [53]  
[2] On the other hand, we, as professional outlets such as automotive finishing shops, contribute significant volumes of paint sludge and residues. It is estimated that the paint sludge we generate annually by the automotive industry, due to overspray, is 200,000–500,000 t for both new cars and repaints. [3] Many of us end-users also engage in improper disposal, pouring paint into municipal drains or onto land, leading to pollution of water systems and soil. Current waste-management pathways often fail to safely contain hazardous compounds, allowing toxic metals, volatile organic emissions, and even microplastics to leach into surrounding ecosystems. Incineration emits greenhouse gases and requires costly flue-gas abatement, while landfill disposal represents a loss of valuable material. Critically, such approaches overlook the latent value embedded in spent paints and sludges. These waste streams are rich in high-performance raw materials such as titanium dioxide, iron oxides, polymeric binders, silica, calcium carbonate, and additives that we can recover and reuse. Advanced material recovery and upcycling technologies can enable their reintegration into new formulations, or diversion to energy-intensive sectors such as cement, ceramics, and glass manufacturing, where they substitute virgin fillers and pigments. Such integrated circular approaches offer dual benefits: reduce our virgin resource dependency and achieve up to 40% reductions in CO₂ emissions per kg of substituted material. They also open new economic avenues for SMEs and heavy industry alike, in line with the goals of the EU’s Circular Economy Action Plan and the Twin Transition. We will deliver an integrated, industrially validated circular system for the paint industry and supply chain, from production to end-users, driving consumer behavioral change by providing a holistic solution to spoiled paints and paint sludges. The approach is co-developed and validated with key industrial partners across the value chain, including PPG Industries (paint production), CRF/Stellantis (automotive coating applications), Torrecid (ceramics and glazing), and Farplas (industrial paint waste streams), ensuring direct industrial relevance and uptake pathways.  
RePaintIT is implemented via an integrated three-layer system combining digital traceability, advanced processing technologies, and circular material valorisation:  
1. RePaintIT Platform – Digital Circularity Layer  
   - Digital marketplace linking households, workshops, recyclers, and manufacturers  
   - Real-time tracking of waste volumes, locations, and composition, facilitating logistics optimisation  
   - Decision Support Tool (DST) for optimizing routing of both collected and processed paints  
   - Integration with the EU Digital Product Passport (DPP), ensuring transparent batch-level data (composition, VOCs, biocides) and regulatory compliance across the value chain  
2. Purification & Processing Lines – Technological Core  
   - Microwave with embedded ultrasound system (MW/US)  
   - Pulsed electric fields for rapid bacterial decontamination (PEF)  
   - Functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) for spoiled paints purification (F-MNPs)  
   - Solvent-assisted mechanochemical separation (SAMS) for binder and pigment liberation  
3. Re-use, Re-purposing & Upcycling Routes – Valorization Layer  
   - Re-use or direct feed of recovered pigments/fillers into new waterborne paints  
   - Diversion of recovered materials to cement, ceramics/glazing, and polymeric tile industries  

Specific Objectives - SO

1.1 Objectives and ambition Our main objective in INFUSE is to develop circular and sustainable -by-design high -entropy alloys with reduced critical-raw-material dependency for high-temperature mobility and turbomachinery applications, by mapping the alloy design space through combinatorial PVD thin -film libraries, using multi -fidelity ML to transfer thin-film predictions to bulk behaviour, and qualifying selected candidates in laser powder bed fusion. All powder that cannot re-enter LPBF production is upcycled into PVD sputter targets that feed back into our alloy screening, or valorised as thermal-spray protective coatings, closing the material loop between discovery and manufacturing. A FAIR data platform, explainable AI, and safe -and-sustainable-by-design gates govern our workflow from alloy design through three TRL 6 demonstrators to European market uptake. We pursue six specific, interlinked objectives that collectively span the full innovation cycle demanded by the call, from circular alloy design to industrial demonstrators, and that are structured so that each delivers independently verifiable outcomes while feeding into the next: O1. Circular Design of HEA: We will define the alloy design strategy and scientific foundations for a circular -by- design development programme targeting Co-free CCA families (Al–Cr–Fe–Ni, Fe–Cr–Al) as Branch A and RHEA families (Ti –Zr–Nb–Ta) as Branch B. The CCA families are selected for their demonstrated oxidation resistance at 800 –1000 °C and proven LPBF printability, while addressing European CRM dependencies and reducing reliance on cobalt and critical refractory elements. Our INFUSE design space is organised as a dual-track architecture. Branch A comprises Co-free, oxidation-resistant CCA families (Al–Cr–Fe–Ni and Fe–Cr–Al with controlled Mn and Si), whose Al and Cr content provides protective Al₂O₃/Cr₂O₃ oxide -scale formation relevant for turbomachinery platform sections and moderate -temperature service (800–1000 °C); these families also offer the highest circularity potential (Co-free, low- CRM, LPBF-processable). Branch B comprises targeted Refractory HEA (RHEA) families (e.g. Ti –Zr– Nb–Ta) that address the higher-temperature regime and strengthen the scientific credibility of the turbine-relevant-environment demonstrator (DEM1). Both branches enter the same DBTL screening funnel (WP2–WP5), where circularity, CRM exposure, SSbD compliance, and processability are screening and ranking outcomes—not blanket assumptions. A CRM-exposure and SSbD gate (WP9) determines which Branch B candidates advance to demonstrator scale. This dual-track approach avoids premature narrowing while maintaining call-compliant relevant-environment ambition. The named composition families serve as initial design-space entry points; PVD combinatorial pre-screening (O2) is expected to refine, extend, or re-scope the active composition regions before candidates advance to LPBF validation. The scope covers composition search space definition, functional performance targets (mechanical strength, oxidation resistance, thermal stability) and printability constraints for laser powder bed fusion. SSbD hazard and circularity are applied as hard gates from the first design cycle to ensure that no candidate advances to experimental screening without satisfying both performance and sustainability criteria. All experimental and computational outcomes, including negative and inconclusive results, are curated with mandatory FAIR metadata and entered into our shared knowledge base to guide and progressively constrain successive DBTL iterations across O2 and O3. O2. Circular high -throughput material screening by PVD: We will build a reusable combinatorial thin -film screening capability through co-sputtering PVD to rapidly explore the alloy search space defined in O1 before committing resources to bulk additive manufacturing. Multiple elemental and compound targets are co-sputtered in configurable multi -target arrangements to produce thin-film compositional libraries with continuous or stepped composition gradients, thereby covering tens to hundreds of alloy variants per deposition campaign. Additionally, off-specification AM powders are consolidated into sputter targets, provided that their composition and purity allow for it. These targets effectively supplement our classical co-sputtering programme while extending the circular material loop of O3 directed to the discovery phase, thereby reducing resource use and decreasing the environmental impact. Each library substrate is systematically characterised for phase constitution, microstructure, hardness, elastic modulus, and oxidation and thermal response relevant to high‑temperature mobility applications, thereby producing composition –property maps that guide selection. These composition-property maps directly inform candidate down-selection to bulk LPBF processing in O3. To reduce the number of experiments, we apply explainable, uncertainty‑aware ML models to target regions of steep property variation and prioritise measurements accordingly. We aim to fuse inexpensive, high‑throughput experiments and simulations to steer and minimise costlier experiments via multi‑fidelity ML. The resulting high-quality library datasets constitute a persistent, FAIR-deposited experimental resource for the European HEA research community. O3. Circular innovative advanced materials by LPBF: We will qualify selected HEA/RHEA candidates for bulk additive manufacturing under circular feedstock conditions by establishing evidence-based LPBF parameters: process windows and quantitative powder governance protocols for the full powder lifecycle. Process window optimisation relates to laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, and layer thickness against build quality for both virgin - and reused -powder conditions. Controlling and correlating these parameters is crucial in fostering inter-laboratory reproducibility. Acceptance criteria are tied to defect density, compositional homogeneity, and mechanical performance. Powder governance defines batch routing thresholds from measured descriptors, including particle morphology, size distribution, chemistry drift, and oxygen content. A three-route valorisation architecture directs reuse-grade batches back to either LPBF production, off-specification batches to PVD target fabrication under O2, or otherwise waste fractions to thermal spray for the DEM2 demonstrator in O6. Every routing decision is recorded in the provenance ledger, ensuring full traceability of material flows and establishing the first validated quantitative circular powder governance protocol for refractory HEA alloys under performance-critical AM conditions in Europe. O4. Technology uptake and transferability: We will quantify the fidelity relationship between thin-film PVD screening data from O2 and bulk LPBF behaviour from O3. We will translate the results into transferability criteria and confidence boundaries that our industrial and SME partners can use to adopt high-throughput alloy screening without repeating full -scale bulk campaigns. Paired datasets on shared alloy compositions, produced in both thin-film and bulk forms under a controlled characterisation protocol. These protocols are used to determine systematic property offsets and variances between the two domains, identifying where PVD data are sufficient to guide bulk alloy selection and where LPBF validation remains mandatory, and extending transferability analysis to High-Velocity Oxy-Fuel (HVOF) thermal-spray coatings produced from valorised waste powder to support the DEM2 coating demonstrator. Validated transferability protocols, open training datasets, and harmonised reporting templates are actively disseminated. This allows our industrial and SME partners across the European mobility and advanced manufacturing sectors to lower the adoption barrier for combinatorial discovery methods and support the broader uptake of circular innovative advanced material practices in the European industrial base. The transferability calibration data additionally serve as input features for downstream ML models, providing composition-property offset descriptors that encode manufacturing-route sensitivity and can be reused in independent optimisation campaigns. O5. FAIR Digital Infrastructure and Explainable AI Platform: We will deliver an open-core Materials Intelligence Platform (MIP) that enforces FAIR-by-design data practices, mandatory metadata schemas, provenance ledgers and conformance validators across all our INFUSE workflows. The MIP provides explainable AI modules for composition selection, powder routing, process window recommendation, and transferability assessment. In full compliance with Article 13 of the EU AI Act, all model recommendations include uncertainty estimates, applicability domain indicators, and human-readable rationales, enabling transparent and auditable human -in-the-loop decision -making throughout our innovation chain. The MIP is pre-loaded with curated public HEA datasets from Zenodo, Materials Cloud, and partner repositories. It is released as a persistent community infrastructure asset interoperable with the European Open Science Cloud (EOSC). This ensures that our INFUSE data, models, and workflows remain findable, reusable, and sovereign after project completion — delivering lasting open-science value to the European research community. O6. TRL 6 Demonstration and business models applied to mobility: We will validate the integrated circular -digital INFUSE workflow at TRL 6 through three sector-relevant prototype demonstrators and establish credible business models and exploitation pathways for the European mobility and advanced manufacturing sectors. DEM1 delivers a circular -powder LPBF prototype of a CRM-reduced CCA/HEA or RHEA turbomachinery platform-section component. This component is validated under high-temperature cyclic oxidation and thermal cycling representative of turbomachinery environments, with a fully auditable powder provenance chain. DEM2 produces a thick HVOF thermal-spray protective coating from valorised waste powder, validated under the same thermal environment, demonstrating the commercial case for coating-from-waste valorisation. DEM3 demonstrates circular valorisation of PVD coatings by depositing HEA-based thin-film strain and temperature sensors on flexible polyimide substrates for real-time Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) of mobility components. Polyimide (Kapton HN) substrates are rated for continuous service up to approximately 400 °C; the target operating envelope for DEM3 sensors is the moderate-temperature range (RT – 200 °C), suitable for cold-section turbine monitoring and automotive powertrain health monitoring, not hot-gas-path service. For higher-temperature regimes, ceramic substrate variants are foreseen as a future option beyond the project scope. The demonstrator is bounded by a formal transferability dossier linking

1.1 Objectives and ambition Introduction: We will design, develop, and validate a reversible energy storage concept based on Protonic-Ceramic Electrochemical Cells (PCECs), operating both as a water electrolyser (on-grid) and a fuel cell (off-grid)1, to provide secure, long-duration energy storage for critical infrastructure in non-interconnected or compromised grid contexts. Our proposed system is based on a reversible protonic ceramic electrochemical cell (rPCEC) stack integrated within a high-temperature module, enabling bidirectional energy conversion between electricity and hydrogen. The system operates alternately in electrolysis mode (hydrogen production) and fuel cell mode (power generation), forming a closed and flexible energy conversion loop. In normal operation (on-grid mode), we will function in electrolysis mode, using electricity from the grid and/or renewable sources to convert water/steam into hydrogen and oxygen within the reversible PCEC stack. The produced hydrogen will subsequently be compressed and stored as a medium-to-long-term energy vector, enabling energy shifting and efficient storage of excess electricity. Simultaneously, oxygen will be generated as a by-product, which can be either stored or directly utilised within the connected infrastructure, depending on operational needs. This mode supports grid flexibility and sector coupling by converting intermittent electrical energy into storable chemical energy. Under off-grid or emergency conditions, we will switch to fuel cell mode, converting stored hydrogen into electricity and heat. The generated electricity will be conditioned through the power electronics subsystem and supplied to critical infrastructure, ensuring continuity of operation. In this mode, the electrochemical reaction is inherently exothermic, producing heat that contributes to maintaining the operating temperature of the stack and can be further utilised to improve overall system efficiency or support local thermal demands. Our system operates at high temperature (~500–650 °C), and thermal stability is maintained once steady-state conditions are reached. Heat generation and reuse are intrinsically coupled with the electrochemical processes, reducing external energy requirements. During electrolysis operation, we can control the system under endothermic, exothermic, or thermally neutral regimes, with thermally neutral operation being optimal for maximising efficiency and minimising external heat input. A hybrid architecture is implemented to ensure operational flexibility and resilience. We will include a battery-based subsystem primarily to support auxiliary systems during off-grid operation, such as hydrogen and oxygen compression units, balance-of-plant components, and power electronics. Since the PCEC stack remains at operating temperature during continuous operation, the battery is not required for stack start-up under steady-state conditions, but rather to guarantee the operation of secondary subsystems that require electrical support and to provide fast-response capability during transient events. 1 In practice, we will operate flexibly across grid-connected and islanded conditions, including the provision of grid services; the on-grid/off-grid distinction is used here to describe the primary operational roles under normal and resilience-driven conditions. HORIZON-CL5-2026-03-D3-21 Novel solutions for off-grid storage of renewable energy for critical infrastructures Page 2 of 45 Overall, our system enables an integrated energy storage and conversion solution capable of supporting grid balancing, peak shaving, and ensuring resilience of critical infrastructures. The co-production of hydrogen and oxygen further enhances our system's versatility, allowing their storage or direct utilisation within the infrastructure, thus improving resource efficiency and system integration. Current backup solutions rely on batteries or diesel generators. Batteries offer limited autonomy, while diesel generates conflict with decarbonisation and air-quality goals. Other off-grid solutions, such as PV+battery systems or thermal storage, can support resilience but are typically limited in duration, controllability or reliability under prolonged outages. Hydrogen-based systems provide scalable, long-duration storage, aligning with EU energy security and resilience priorities. Figure 1. Schematic of H2SAVE system. Our H2SAVE solution (figure 1) consists of a modular Protonic-conducting Ceramic (PCEC) system that: 1. Operates in on-grid electrolyser mode to store renewable energy as hydrogen. 2. Objectives of the proposed work and pertinence to the work programme topic: Our overall objective of H2SAVE is to design, optimise, and validate a resilient hydrogen-based hybrid energy system capable of ensuring a reliable power supply for critical infrastructures while supporting the decarbonisation of the energy system. Our proposed solution integrates reversible electrochemical conversion technologies, hybrid energy storage, intelligent energy management systems, and digital twin modelling tools to enable flexible, resilient operation under both grid-connected and islanded conditions. Our ambition is to move beyond conventional backup energy systems by delivering a fully integrated hydrogen-based hybrid architecture capable of combining energy storage, grid services, and resilient backup functionality within a single platform, thereby addressing the growing need for secure, low-carbon energy supply solutions for critical infrastructure, including industrial sites, energy communities, and distributed microgrids. 1.1 Objectives and ambition Our overall aim with FLAME is to develop and deploy the FLAME-Platform for microbiome-based ecosystem analytics and evidence-based intervention guidance in a One Health context. This includes detecting shifts in microbiomes during food production that precede loss of system stability and increased contamination risks, identifying soil and farm microbiome changes linked to transmission dynamics across the food system, and applying dietary interventions to restore gut microbiome functions. We are the first project in the world to establish a quantitative and predictive framework for microbiome research, integrating system-level concepts with multi-omics data and AI-enabled analysis and prediction. It will provide the basis for public health authorities and regulators to monitor microbiome stability and emerging risks, support farmers in managing agricultural disruptions, enable food processors and SMEs to steer microbial processes and reduce contamination and losses, and empower advisors and operators to implement future-proof management strategies. In parallel, we and European research infrastructures (RIs) will benefit from reusable data, standardised protocols and analytical frameworks, while digital solution providers and the biotech sector can build on our models, strain libraries and defined microbial communities (DMCs). FLAME will also support policymakers, standardisation bodies and One Health networks in embedding microbiome-based approaches and enable health-related stakeholders to detect early signals of microbial system destabilisation across interconnected domains. Together, these advances position microbiomes as actionable control variables for improving stability, safety and sustainability across food systems. Over the past decade, our research has generated unprecedented insights into the composition and dynamics of microbiomes across environmental, animal, and human ecosystems, which together form the backbone of food production. It is estimated that global investment in microbiome research has surpassed US$20 billion. Yet despite this progress, microbiome research has largely evolved within discipline and sectoral silos, limiting the transferability of insights across environments, hosts, or food systems. There is increasing evidence that microbiomes regulate key biological processes across the farm-to-fork continuum, shaping ecosystem functioning, host physiology, immune regulation, and pathogen transmission. In adaptive states, microbiomes promote resilience, whereas under stress they may become drivers of vulnerability, pathogen transmission and systemic disruption. The influence of microbiomes extends to both global and individual health, crop protection, and soil restoration. Microbiomes are also further recognised as determinants of stability in natural and managed food systems. However, what remains missing is an operational cross-ecosystem framework that translates microbiome observations into measurable microbiome states (adaptive, stressed, maladaptive) and actionable system-level interventions. FLAME shifts microbiome research from fragmented datasets towards an integrated, predictive and steerable systems approach to food-system resilience. By combining time-resolved multi-omics data and metadata with defined system states representing functional and dysfunctional microbial conditions, we enable quantitative analysis and control of microbiome-driven processes across the agrifood system. To realise our ambition, we exploit three experimentally grounded and mechanistically complementary cases as models for the agro-ecosystem, food production and human gut spanning the farm-to-fork continuum (FLAME-Cases 1-3). These FLAME-Cases are connected through a shared hypothesis: “Microbiome stability relies on general ecological principles that are driven by functional coexistence as responses to perturbations and recovery dynamics.” Modern food systems span interconnected domains from agroecosystems to food processing and human nutrition. To capture this complexity, we establish three complementary FLAME-Cases that together represent key segments of the farm-to-fork continuum. FLAME-Case 1 provides the primary system-level model capturing microbiome interconnectedness across agroecosystems, spanning soil, plant and animal compartments. FLAME-Case 2 is a continuation of FLAME case 1 and aims for stability measurements in the food processing sphere. FLAME-Case 3 moves our research to the human nutrition field. All FLAME-Cases are harmonised through shared experimental protocols (WP1), sampling and metadata standards (WP2), metagenomics and metabolomics (WP5, 6, 7 and 9) and common AI frameworks (WP3 and WP8). In detail, FLAME-Case 1 (Agro-Ecosystem Resilience) investigates microbiome-mediated stability across the soil-plant-feed-animal-farmer-product-wastewater interface. It longitudinally compares microbial dynamics and community-level resilience mechanisms in regenerative agriculture (RegAg) farming systems compared to intensive farming systems across three European regions (southern, central and northwestern Europe). Sampling campaigns are conducted across seasons, including periods characterised by heat stress and water scarcity. FLAME-Case 1 identifies adaptive versus maladaptive microbiome states. FLAME-Case 2 (Food Processing & Production Resilience) builds on the Europe-wide MASTER baseline dataset (EU Horizon 2020, see Table 2), where ~2,000 samples from food and food-processing environments across sectors and EU countries were analysed. Core microbiomes of European food processing facilities will be leveraged from the data already available and representative DMCs will be developed from the MASTER partner’s microbial strain collections (part of this consortium). DMCs are experimentally exposed to four selected industrial stressors, already defined in consultation with food industry stakeholders, including raw-material quality shifts coming from FLAME Case-1, changes in disinfectants, pathogen introduction and plastics contamination to quantify the tipping points where microbiomes unrecoverably shift to a maladaptive state. Predictions derived from laboratory experiments are subsequently tested in two selected European processing facilities previously characterised in MASTER. FLAME-Case 3 (Dietary Modulation & Human Gut Resilience Model) establishes and investigates controlled dietary perturbations to quantify microbiome adaptation under nutritional shifts, such as switching from a meat-based diet to a high-fibre plant-based diet. Using validated ex vivo gut systems and functional assays, FLAME-Case 3 identifies adaptive (recoverable), transitional (shifting towards maladaptive) and maladaptive (non-recoverable) microbiome states, reflecting the capacity of the gut microbiome to reorganise. In detail, we deliver novel elements of a conceptual One Health framework in agrifood science: We deliver the harmonised, data-driven FLAME-Platform, integrating cross-ecosystem, time-resolved multi-omics datasets across previously disconnected compartments of the agrifood chain, which serve as model input and continuously feed the FLAME-Hub. It is enabled by standardised protocols and interoperable analytical workflows. The FLAME-Platform consists of: (i) Deployable AI-enabled decision-support tools for predictive monitoring, scenario exploration and microbial adaptation in food systems. These include trained models, digital twins, reusable software components and user interfaces, notably generative AI models (FLAME-AIs) and a data-analysing “Virtual Microbiome Bioinformatician” (FLAME-VMB), developed with the start-up Galagos. The FLAME-VBM enables natural-language interaction with complex microbiome analytics. This directly addresses a major barrier for companies, particularly SMEs, which lack the analytical capacity to interpret microbiome data, by providing automated, accessible tools for microbiome-driven decision-making. (ii) The FLAME-Hub, a state-of-the-art communication and analytics environment evolving from the FoodSafeR Open Digital Hub, serving as the central integration and user access point and enabling interaction with FLAME-AIs (deployed via the hub), FLAME-VMB and the FLAME-Map. (iii) The FLAME-Map, a graph-based framework linking microbial taxa, functions, metabolites and contextual drivers, enabling identification of microbial adaptation, system-state transitions and early-warning signals, and supporting cross-case comparison and AI-driven prediction of microbiome stability and disruption. Structured study and metadata management systems (e.g. SODAR) underpin harmonised data organisation. All components are delivered as modular Application Programming Interface (API)-enabled and FAIR-compliant digital resources ensuring interoperability with European Research Infrastructures (RIs). Importantly, the FLAME-Hub is initially populated with curated and harmonised existing datasets (identified in WP2), providing an immediate data framework for model training. This ensures a smooth start of project activities from day one of project commencement. The FLAME-Cases then generate experimental data, enabling the application of previously trained models on novel, lab-derived datasets. As the FLAME-Hub is flexibly designed to be expandable and scalable, it will lend itself to the future integration of additional microbiome data beyond the project duration. The FLAME-Hub will also support concrete applications, for example the monitoring of antimicrobial and antifungal resistance risks, optimisation of hygiene practices in food processing, mitigation of risks, and evidence-based design of sustainable farming and dietary strategies. While policy frameworks (e.g., EC’s Building the Future with Nature: Boosting Biotechnology and Biomanufacturing in the EU; EU Biodiversity Strategy for 2030; the R&I Food 2030 framework) recognise the relevance of microbiomes for innovation and sustainability, fragmentation of data, lack of harmonised standards, and limited interoperability between RIs continue to constrain impact and limit the effective use and integration of microbiome data. We will align our data generation, annotation and sharing workflows with established RIs, including ELIXIR (for standards, tools and interoperable multi-omics and metadata management), MIRRI and DSMZ (for microbial resource access, strain deposition and characterisation). FLAME’s ambitions will be realised through eight S.M.A.R.T. objectives with verifiable KPIs. We will deliver substantial capacity building and long-term skills development. By embedding tutorials within live experimental workflows, AI infrastructures and decision-support environments (FLAME-Hub), as well as targeted training courses, hands-on workshops and co-creation formats, we will contribute to the emergence of a new generation of researchers and practitioners working at the interface of microbiome science, systems ecology, AI and food-system innovation.
